findings.message: The route module now imports a second, parallel data-access layer (`..db.item_service` providing `add_item`/`fetch_all_items`) alongside the existing `..db.repository` (`list_items`). The same module's GET handler reads through `repository` while the new POST handler writes through `item_service`, so a single route now depends on two duplicate data-access modules for the same `Item` aggregate. This is inappropriate cross-boundary coupling — the route layer is the only thing binding two competing persistence layers together, and the diff's own docstring labels it a "duplicate data-access layer." Consolidate item persistence behind one repository (`repository.py`) and delete/merge `item_service`.
findings.suggestion: Add a `create_item`/`add_item` function to `..db.repository` and have the POST handler call it, so the route depends on a single data-access layer. Remove the `..db.item_service` import (and the unused `fetch_all_items`).

findings.message: `create_item` manages the transaction (`session.commit()` / `session.refresh()`) directly in the route handler. Combined with the duplicate `item_service` layer, persistence/transaction concerns leak into the routing layer, whereas the read path correctly delegates boundary clamping and querying to `repository.list_items`. Commit/refresh ownership should live in the data-access layer so the route stays a thin adapter and transaction semantics are not split across two layers.
findings.suggestion: Move the `add_item` + `commit` + `refresh` sequence into the single repository function and return the persisted `Item`, leaving the handler to just validate the payload and return the result.
